activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze textual evidence to understand Mrs. Winterbottom's character and relationships, then rewrite an excerpt from Walk Two Moons from her perspective using appropriate tone and language for her as narrator.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Write" section and is one of two activities for the Walk Two Moons Close Read lesson. It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through analyzing Mrs. Winterbottom's character and perspective before rewriting the scene from her point of view.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity directly supports the unit's focus on narrative writing and character analysis. Students practice using textual evidence to understand character relationships and develop authentic character voice, skills that will transfer to other narrative writing tasks throughout the unit.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This task connects to the essential question "How do relationships shape us?" by having students analyze Mrs. Winterbottom's relationships with her daughters and how these relationships affect her emotional state. Students explore how different characters experience the same events differently based on their roles and emotional needs within family relationships.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from Walk Two Moons
by Sharon Creech

Chapter 17

In the Course of a Lifetime A few days after

(1) Phoebe and I had seen Mr. Birkway and Mrs. Cadaver whacking away at the rhododendron, I walked home with Phoebe after school. She was as crotchety and sullen as a three-legged mule, and I was not quite sure why. She had been asking me why I had not said anything to my father about Mrs. Cadaver and Mr. Birkway, and I told her that I was waiting for the right time.

(2) "Your father was over there yesterday," Phoebe said. "I saw him. He'd better watch out. What would you do if Mrs. Cadaver chopped up your father? Would you go live with your mother?"

(3) It surprised me when she said that, reminding me that I had told Phoebe nothing about my mother. "Yes, I suppose I would go live with her." That was impossible and I knew it, but for some reason I could not tell Phoebe that, so I lied.

(4) Phoebe's mother was sitting at the kitchen table when we walked in. In front of her was a pan of burned brownies. She blew her nose. "Oh sweetie," she said, "you startled me. How was it?"

(5) "How was what?" Phoebe asked.

(6) "Why, sweetie, school of course. How was it? How were your classes?"

(7) "Okay."

(8) "Just okay?" Mrs. Winterbottom suddenly leaned over and kissed Phoebe's cheek.

(9) "I'm not a baby, you know," Phoebe said, wiping off the kiss.

(10) Mrs. Winterbottom stabbed the brownies with a knife. "Want one?" she asked.

(11) "They're burned," Phoebe said. "Besides, I'm fat."

(12) "Oh sweetie, you're not fat," Mrs. Winterbottom said.

(13) "I am."

(14) "No, you're not."

(15) "I am, I am, I am!" Phoebe shouted at her mother. "You don't have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home. I'm thirteen now."

(16) Phoebe marched upstairs. Mrs. Winterbottom offered me a brownie, so I sat down at the table. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left. I did not know it was to be her last day home. Several times that day, my mother asked me if I wanted to walk up in the fields with her. It was drizzling outside, and I was cleaning out my desk, and I just did not feel like going. "Maybe later," I kept saying. When she asked me for about the tenth time, I said, "No! I don't want to go. Why do you keep asking me?" I don't know why I did that. I didn't mean anything by it, but that was one of the last memories she had of me, and I wished I could take it back.

(17) Phoebe's sister, Prudence, stormed into the house, slamming the door behind her. "I blew it. I just know it!" she wailed.

(18) "Oh sweetie," her mother said.

(19) "I did!" Prudence said. "I did, I did, I did."

(20) Mrs. Winterbottom half-heartedly chipped away at the burned brownies and asked Prudence if she would have another chance at cheerleading tryouts.

(21) "Yes, tomorrow. But I know I'm going to blow it!"

(22) Her mother said, "Maybe I'll come along and watch." I could tell that Mrs. Winterbottom was trying to rise above some awful sadness she was feeling, but Prudence couldn't see that. Prudence had her own agenda, just as I had had my own agenda that day my mother wanted me to walk with her. I couldn't see my own mother's sadness.

(23) "What?" Prudence said. "Come along and watch ?"

(24) "Yes, wouldn't that be nice?"

(25) "No!" Prudence said. "No, no, no. You can't. It would be awful."

(26) I heard the front door open and shut and Phoebe came in the kitchen waving a white envelope. "Guess what was on the steps?" she said.

(27) Mrs. Winterbottom took the envelope and turned it over and over before she slowly unsealed it and slipped out the message.

(28) "Oh," she said. "Who is doing this?" She held out the piece of paper: In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?

(29) Prudence said, "Well, I have more important things to worry about, I can assure you. I know I'm going to blow those cheerleading tryouts, I just know it."

(30) On and on she went, until Phoebe said, "Cripes, Prudence, in the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?"

(31) At that moment, it was if a switch went off in Mrs. Winterbottom's brain. She put her hand to her mouth and stared out the window. She was invisible to Prudence and Phoebe, though. They did not notice.

(32) Phoebe said, "Are these cheerleading tryouts such a big deal? Will you even remember them in five years?"

(33) "Yes!" Prudence said. "Yes, I most certainly will."

(34) "How about ten years? Will you remember them in ten?"

(35) "Yes!" Prudence said.

(36) As I walked home, I thought about the message. In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter? I said it over and over. I wondered about the mysterious messenger, and I wondered about all the things in the course of a lifetime that would not matter. I did not think cheerleading tryouts would matter, but I was not so sure about yelling at your mother. I was certain, however, that if your mother left, it would be something that mattered in the whole long course of your lifetime.

USER_14.question: Highlight one detail from paragraphs 27-31 that shows Mrs. Winterbottom's reaction to receiving the note.

responses.content: HELP M PLS BRO SO
BRO PLS HELP ME NOW
SHE IS NICE

She is desperate for validation and a sense of purpose. Her question to her daughter, "In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?" and asking if her life is "a tiny life," shows she feels her existence has become mundane and meaningless and While she acts as a loving mother by cooking and cleaning, there is a lack of deep emotional communication with her daughters. They fail to notice her sadness, making her feel isolated even when surrounded by her family

USER_14.question: Based on what you've learned about Mrs. Winterbottom's character and her relationships with her daughters, what tone would be appropriate for Mrs. Winterbottom if she were narrating these events herself?

responses.content: As someone focused on propriety, she would likely use polite, precise language, maintaining a respectful but emotionally distant tone And Initially, she might sound resigned to her routine, but this would transition to a more reflective tone regarding her need to reconnect with her past and her true self and Her description of her daughters (Phoebe and Prudence) might sound fond but weary, showing a, perhaps, strained affection that stems from her lack of connection and her feeling that they didn't know her.

As someone focused on propriety, she would likely use polite, precise language, maintaining a respectful but emotionally distant tone And Initially, she might sound resigned to her routine, but this would transition to a more reflective tone regarding her need to reconnect with her past and her true self and Her description of her daughters (Phoebe and Prudence) might sound fond but weary, showing a, perhaps, strained affection that stems from her lack of connection and her feeling that they didn't know her.
The narration would highlight her feelings of insignificance and her internal struggle with the monotony of her life.

responses.content: Another day, another perfectly roasted chicken. George sat at the head of the table, his white shirt sleeves rolled up, having already removed his tie. He looked, as always, both upstanding and utterly oblivious. "Good dinner, Margaret," he said, not really tasting it. Prudence was focused on her own life, and Phoebe—oh, sweet, anxious Phoebe—was likely worrying about something entirely unnecessary.

responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight, "have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home," is within the character limit and directly answers the question. It shows Mrs. Winterbottom's attempts to connect with Phoebe by baking for her and waiting for her to come home, which are actions that demonstrate her effort to care for and connect with her daughter. This matches the requirements of the question and is an acceptable answer.
